coredata.dc:title: Optical properties of carbon nanodots synthesized by laser induced fragmentation of graphite powder suspended in water
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Pandey, Nitin
Srivastava, Rajneesh K.
Kumar Singh, Manish
Singh, Jay
coredata.dc:description: Luminescent carbon nanodots (CNDs) have been synthesized by using laser induced fragmentation of graphite powder in deionized water. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image shows that the diameters of the CNDs lie in the range of 30–100nm. The synthesized sample has been characterized by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy and Photoluminescence spectroscopy. The synthesized CNDs exhibited visible, tunable and stable luminescence property. These CNDs have potential future applications such as optoelectronic devices and biosensors. The CNDs having bio-compatibility and bio-absorbability hence it may be used in fluorescence label and microscopy of living organisms.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fragmentation
CNDs
TEM
Graphite

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CNDs synthesized by laser induced fragmentation method.
TEM images CNDs at scale of 200nm.
Photoluminescence spectra of CNDs synthesized by laser induced fragmentation method.
Raman spectra of the CNDs synthesized by laser induced fragmentation method.

head.ce:title: Optical properties of carbon nanodots synthesized by laser induced fragmentation of graphite powder suspended in water

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Luminescent carbon nanodots (CNDs) have been synthesized by using laser induced fragmentation of graphite powder in deionized water. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image shows that the diameters of the CNDs lie in the range of 30–100nm. The synthesized sample has been characterized by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy and Photoluminescence spectroscopy. The synthesized CNDs exhibited visible, tunable and stable luminescence property. These CNDs have potential future applications such as optoelectronic devices and biosensors. The CNDs having bio-compatibility and bio-absorbability hence it may be used in fluorescence label and microscopy of living organisms.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescence in carbon-based nanoparticles are having variety of applications due to their high photo stability, large two-photon excitation cross-sections and photoinduced electron transfers. Carbon nanoparticles are small in size, readily water soluble, low toxic, having low molecular weight and eco friendly and alternatives also used in biological applications [1–5]. Quasi spherical carbon nanoparticles termed as carbon nanodots (CNDs) are recently discovered with size dependent optical and electronic properties. CNDs are good candidates for replacing semiconductor quantum dots because they are superior to them in terms of chemical inertness, stability, toxicity, cost, green synthesis, functionalization and biocompatibility. Due to these excellent properties of CNDs they are having applications in field of biological labeling, life sciences, bio-imaging, drug delivery, chemical sensing and other different optoelectronic device application [6–21]. Further, CNDs with suitable size distribution have potential to replace phosphor coating in white light light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [22,23]. Nevertheless, demonstration of stimulated emission should be the “Holy Grails” of the luminescence CNDs. Capability to support lasing emission is an unambiguous verification for potential use of CNDs in optoelectronics and biological applications. Near-Infrared Region (NIR) photoluminescence study can also be obtained under NIR excitation. NIR photoluminescence excited by NIR excitation are significant and useful for bio nanotechnology because of the transparency of body tissues in the NIR region [24].

Various forms of carbon nanoparticles such as nanotubes, fullerenes, graphene, nanofibers, nanodiamonds [25–38] etc. have been synthesized by different synthesis methods such as laser ablation method, electrochemical oxidation method, chemical oxidation method, thermolysis method, micro assisted method, microwave hydrothermal method, hydrothermal method [39–48] etc. Laser ablation is very efficient physical method for synthesis of nano material [49–51]. This method is green, cheap, easy and versatile for synthesis of various metallic, alloy, semiconductor, metal oxide and many more nanostructures. In this method the target material absorbs the laser light, leading to a local increase in temperature and evaporation of a small amount of material from the surface. Vaporized material is rapidly cooled down by the surrounding liquid. The optical properties of the nanoparticles are strongly affected by the surrounding liquid environment. The liquid environment may cause the dispersed nanocrystals to be unstable in the liquid and effect or even change their properties [52–54].
In the present work, CNDs has been synthesized by laser induced fragmentation of graphite powder (particles size 1–20µm) suspension in double deionized water and optical properties have been studied.

The Graphite powder was mixed in the double deionized water and stirred continuously in a glass tube. An Nd: YAG laser (Spectra Physics, USA) with 1064nm wavelength, operated at 35mJ/pulse energy, 10ns pulse width and 10Hz repetition rate was focused to a spot size of 200µm at the center of the glass tube using 25cm focal length of quartz lens for 60min. The colloidal suspension of material obtained after ablation has been used for further characterization.

A drop from colloidal solution of nanoparticles was placed on a carbon coated copper grid and was dried for Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) analysis. The UV–-vis absorption spectrum of synthesized colloidal solution of nanoparticles was obtained by using Perkin Elmer Lambda 35, double beam spectrophotometer. The photoluminescence and photoluminescence excitation were performed using a Perkin Elmer LS-55 spectrophotometer. The Raman spectra were collected using a micro-Raman Renishaw spectrometer equipped with a charge coupled device detector. Two laser lines were used to excite the samples viz. 514nm (green laser) and 785nm (red laser). All the measurements were performed at room temperature.

Fig. 1 shows the UV–-vis absorption spectrum of CNDs synthesized by laser induced fragmentation of graphite powder suspended in water. It is observed from the UV–-vis absorption spectrum, that absorption peak is at 274nm lying in the absorption band 250–300nm. A similar result has been reported earlier by several authors [24,46,47,55]. Zhang et al. have synthesized graphene quantum dots and reported two absorption peaks around 227nm and 360nm. The typical absorption peak at 227nm could be assigned to the π–π⁎ transition of graphitic sp2 domains and that at 360nm to π–p⁎ absorption band [56]. Hu et al. has also reported the absorption spectrum band at 300nm [57].

Fig. 2 shows the TEM image of CNDs synthesized by laser induced fragmentation of graphite powder suspended in water. TEM image shows the oval like structure of CNDs. The particle size calculated from TEM image lies in the range of 30–100nm.

Fig. 3 shows the photoluminescence emission spectra of CNDs synthesized by laser induced fragmentation of graphite powder suspended in water in the visible range. Photoluminescence is one of the most fascinating and important features of CNDs All photoluminescence spectra were taken in air at room temperature. Photoluminescence spectrum study was carried out by using light of different wavelengths 325nm, 350nm, 375nm, 400nm, 425nm, 450nm, 475nm, 500nm as excitation source of energy. When it was excited by 325nm, different emission wavelengths were found corresponding to the visible emissions of CNDs covering blue-to-red (blue, green, yellow, and red) wavelength range in the same sample. The spectrum also includes emission due to electron–hole recombination at the band edge. As the excitation wavelength was varied from 325nm to 500nm, the peaks corresponding to photoluminescence spectrum with excitation wavelength of 325nm gets red shifted. In addition to these peaks, additional peaks were generated, which were normally absent in the photoluminescence spectrum when it was excited by 325nm were observed. This may be attributed to presence of sub-defect levels, traps inside the band gap and due to presence CNDs of different size. The emission energy and the amplitude of photoluminescence peaks in a given spectrum strongly depend upon the morphology of carbon nanoparticles as well as on the laser excitation energy [58,59].

Fig. 4 shows Raman spectra of CNDs synthesized by laser induced fragmentation of graphite powder suspended in water for two different excitation lasers, green laser (514nm) and red laser (785nm). Fig. 4(a) shows two signature peaks for carbon, D band and G band are present at 1356cm−1 and 1581cm−1 respectively for the excitation with green laser (514nm). As the intensity of G band is more than that of D band, D band corresponds to the disordered structure in the crystalline of the sp2 cluster while G band is the response of the in-plane stretching motion of the symmetric sp2 C–C bonds [8,60]. It shows presence of pristine carbon nanomaterials in the form of CNDs [61–63] and can be assigned to both the structural disorder and the presence of residual oxygen [64]. Fig. 4(b) shows the Raman spectra for excitation with red laser (785nm). The intensity for G-band at 1581cm−1 for red laser increases several times as compared to that for green laser, which further proves presence of pristine carbon nano materials in the form of CNDs

CNDs have been synthesized by laser induced fragmentation of graphite powder suspended in water. The UV–-vis absorption spectrum of synthesized CNDs exhibits absorption peak at 274nm within broad absorption band of 250–300nm. TEM image shows oval like structure of CNDs and particle size calculated from TEM lie in the range of 30–100nm. Synthesized CNDs exhibit the excitation wavelength dependent photoluminescence behavior covering blue-to-red (blue, green, yellow, and red) wavelength range. As the excitation wavelength increases, there is shifting in peak position and some additional peaks are generated. Raman spectrum shows two signature peaks for carbon, D band and G band are present at 1356cm−1 and 1581cm−1, respectively for excitation with green laser (514nm) and shows presence of pristine carbon nanomaterials in the form of CNDs. The intensity for G-band at 1581cm−1 for red laser increases several times as compared to that for green laser.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are thankful to the Director NPL—New Delhi for Photoluminescence and Raman facilities. They are also thankful to Prof. Ram Gopal, University of Allahabad for providing laser facility.
